title: Verilog HDL Introduction
description: Write synthesisable Verilog and see the gate-level netlist generated by Yosys. Explore a 4-bit adder and modify it to understand synthesis.

steps.instruction: The playground loads the pre-written 4-bit ripple-carry adder. Click 'Load Verilog Playground' to download Yosys WASM (~40 MB) and synthesise the design.
steps.hint: Yosys is an open-source synthesis tool. It converts RTL (register-transfer level) Verilog into a gate-level netlist.

steps.instruction: After synthesis, DigitalJS renders an interactive simulation. Toggle the input bits and observe how the output sum and carry change in real time.
steps.hint: The simulation shows actual gates (AND, OR, XOR, FA cells). You can see how Verilog's '+' operator maps to real hardware.

steps.instruction: Modify the code: change 'output [3:0] sum' to 'output [4:0] sum' (5-bit output) and update the assign to 'assign sum = a + b + cin'. Re-synthesise. The carry is now embedded in sum[4].
steps.hint: RTL Verilog uses arithmetic on bit-vectors. The synthesis tool handles the carry chain automatically.

steps.instruction: Try a simple multiplexer: replace the adder with 'module mux2(input sel, input a, input b, output y); assign y = sel ? b : a; endmodule'. Notice Yosys synthesises a single MUX gate.
steps.hint: The ternary operator in Verilog maps directly to a multiplexer in hardware.

steps.instruction: Write a D flip-flop: 'module dff(input clk, input d, output reg q); always @(posedge clk) q <= d; endmodule'. Synthesis will produce a DFF cell — the fundamental building block of sequential logic.
steps.hint: Sequential logic (flip-flops, registers) uses 'always @(posedge clk)' blocks. Combinational logic uses 'assign' or 'always @(*)'.
